data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: TSMC Revenue Surges On Pre-Tariff Rush
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

TSMC Q1 sales top estimates on surging AI demand ahead of U.S. tariffs. (00:22) Amazon (AMZN) pulls the plug on China orders, leaving vendors in the lurch - Bloomberg. (02:03) U.S. Postal Service aims to raise stamp prices to 78 cents. (02:56)

This is an abridged transcript.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE:TSM) reported a nearly 42% surge in Q1 revenue, fueled by strong demand for AI servers and smartphones ahead of looming U.S. tariffs.

First-quarter revenue totaled NT$839.25 billion ($25.6 billion), up 41.6% compared to the same period in 2024, marking the company’s fastest pace of growth since 2022. Analysts on average expected around NT$830.5 billion, as per Bloomberg.

March revenue also rose 46.5% Y/Y to approximately NT$285.96 billion as electronics makers had built up inventories in U.S. warehouses, preparing for possible trade disruptions.

In March, TSMC CEO C.C. Wei and President Donald Trump jointly unveiled a $100 billion expansion in U.S. chipmaking investments, supporting the White House’s push to restore manufacturing. Trump attributed the increased commitment to the impact of higher tariffs.

Taiwan Semiconductor (NYSE:TSM), who customers include Apple (AAPL), Nvidia (NVDA), and AMD (AMD), will report its full Q1 earnings on April 17. Investors will be watching for a possible downward revision to the full-year sales target and capital expenditure plans, as the outlook for global chip demand weakens amid uncertainty following new U.S. tariffs, Bloomberg Intelligence said.

The company expects net revenue in the range of US$25B-US$25.8B (consensus estimate: $25.52B) for 1Q25, with a gross margin of 57%-59%.

Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) has cancelled orders that originate from China and Southeast Asian countries, leaving certain vendors on the hook with unsold inventory.

According to a document viewed by Bloomberg, Amazon appears to be reducing its exposure to U.S. tariffs on imported merchandise from China-based suppliers who provide a “significant portion” of Amazon’s (NASDAQ:AMZN) components and finished goods.

According to Bloomberg, a consultant to Amazon (AMZN) vendors claims the company has cancelled several “direct import orders” of products made in China without warning. Because Amazon (AMZN) acts as the “importer of record” on these orders, it is responsible for paying import tariffs once the merchandise arrives in the U.S. However, if the order is canceled once shipping is initiated, the tariff burden lies with the vendor once the merchandise reaches the U.S.

The United States Postal Service announced proposed price changes for its mailing services, set to take effect on July 13, pending approval from the Postal Regulatory Commission.

Among the most notable changes is a 5-cent increase in the price of a First-Class Mail Forever stamp, raising the cost from 73 cents to 78 cents.

The adjustments, approved by the USPS Board of Governors, represent an average price increase of approximately 7.4% across mailing services.

The agency says the new rates are essential to support its Delivering for America 10-year plan, which aims to stabilize USPS finances and modernize operations.

In addition to letter and postcard rates, the USPS is proposing adjustments to Special Services, including a 12% reduction in postal insurance rates — a rare cost decrease that may benefit shippers and online retailers.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Google reiterates $75B spending plan for data centers amid tariff woes

Former Treasury secretary Summers says govt 'rightly scared after collapsing markets'

EU said to weigh buying more U.S. gas, but green transition still in focus

Catalyst watch:

  • Walmart (WMT) will hold its annual shareholder meeting.

  • TD Synnex (SNX) will hold its Investor Day event.

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is down 2.8% at $60/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.7% at $82,000. Gold is up 0.8% at $3,111.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is up 3.9% and the DAX is up 5.5%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Constellation Brands (NYSE:STZ) -4% – Shares slipped despite a Q4 beat, as the company’s FY2026 outlook disappointed investors amid trade policy uncertainty.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am CPI

  • 8:30 am Jobless Claims

  • 8:30 am Federal Reserve Bank President Thomas Barkin will speak on"Driving Through the Economic Fog" and participate in a moderated question-and-answer session before the Talbot County Business Appreciation Summit sponsored by the Talbot County Chamber of Commerce.

  • 10:00 am Kansas City Federal Reserve Bank President Jeffrey Schmid will speak on the economic outlook and monetary policy before the Secured Finance Network Independent Finance Roundtable 2025.

  • 12:00 pm Chicago Federal Reserve Bank President Austan Goolsbee participates in a moderated question-and-answer session before the Economic Club of New York.

  • 12:00 pm Philadelphia Federal Reserve Bank President Patrick Harker speaks on"Fintech" before the 2025 Fintech and Financial Institutions Research Conference.

data.title: Voya Core Plus Fixed Income SMA Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

A total return approach, investing across the full spectrum of the fixed income market, including up to 20% in below investment grade securities.

Market review

Strong economic data continued to come through in the final quarter of 2024, which had the impact of driving spreads tighter while reversing the rate rally experienced in 3Q24.

The gross domestic product (GDP) report for 3Q24 once again came in elevated (initially reporting at 2.8% and later revised to 3.1%) driven largely by strong consumer and government spending, which grew at 3.7% and 5.1%, respectively. Notably, consumer spending accounted for roughly 80% of the overall growth, highlighting its critical role in the economy.

Inflationary pressures remained a key concern. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) surprised to the upside in early October, and while subsequent data showed stability in both CPI and Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) inflation, both remained elevated. Core PCE, the U.S. Federal Reserve’s preferred measure of inflation, finished the quarter at 2.8% year over year, slightly above where it was trending in the summer. Notably, core goods prices, which had experienced a deflationary trend for several months, began to reaccelerate.

Labor market dynamics continued to show signs of softening without entering a state of deterioration. Job gains remained reasonable, and although the unemployment rate resumed its upward trajectory, it remained below the high watermark set earlier in the year. Meanwhile, wage growth remained strong, which helps explain both the resilience of consumer spending and the stickiness of services inflation.

While the Fed continued to deliver rate cuts at both the November and December meetings, the December cut was accompanied by more "hawkish" elements. Specifically, the Fed’s Dot plot indicated only two cuts projected for 2025 — down from four in the previous iteration. Additionally, officials moved their projection for both growth and inflation higher, while their projection for the unemployment rate moved lower and only slightly above the current level.

Overall, the fourth quarter of 2024 was characterized by resilient labor market dynamics, strong economic growth and sticky inflation. The impact on fixed income performance was mixed, with yields rising and credit spreads tightening, leading to modestly negative total returns for most fixed income sectors.

For the quarter, the Voya Core Plus Fixed Income SMA outperformed the Index on gross-of-fees, but slightly underperformed on net-of-fees basis. Sector allocation, duration and yield curve decisions contributed, while security selection decisions detracted. Our overweight to credit and underweight

in Treasuries broadly contributed. Our allocation to non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) and credit risk transfers (CRT) was a strong contributor as strong borrower characteristics continued to drive spreads tighter. Similarly, our overweight to investment grade (IG) corporates was also a meaningful contributor but was offset by negative security selection results due to our higher quality bias relative to the benchmark. Meanwhile, positive security selection results in commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) were realized due to our bias towards higher yielding tranches. Finally, due to our shorter duration profile relative to the benchmark, the move higher in rates contributed to relative performance.

Looking forward, we continue to expect economic growth will remain resilient. The recent election outcome further strengthens this view due to election optimism and productivity gains from deregulation. Meanwhile, we expect inflation to resume its downward trend, driven by moderating service prices. In this area, however, the election result lowers our conviction as an adverse policy mix of deficit spending, tariffs and stricter immigration limits would sustain higher levels of demand while constraining supply. That said, we believe a negative market reaction would likely compel the administration to temper those policies before they derail growth.

Assuming this is the case, and inflation does not reaccelerate, we expect the Fed will tolerate inflation that is slightly above their target and deliver a few more rate cuts in an effort to preserve the current cycle.

Overall, this backdrop should be favorable for credit sectors; however, we continue to favor high-quality, shorter-dated assets given where valuations are. Over the quarter, we reduced our overweight to IG corporates and are now neutral to the benchmark from a spread duration perspective. Similarly, we maintain only a modest allocation to high yield (HY) corporates, while high quality securitized makes up much of our active risk. If there is a misstep in policy implementation, we believe we are well positioned to capitalize on the short-lived market dislocation and will look to add market risk further out the curve. Finally, in response to the move higher in rates, we increased the strategy’s duration and are now neutral to the benchmark.

Disclaimers

The Bloomberg US Aggregate Index is composed of US securities in Treasury, government-related, corporate, and securitized sectors that are of investment-grade quality or better, have at least one year to maturity and have an outstanding par value of at least $250 million. Indexes do not reflect fees, brokerage commissions, taxes or other expenses of investing. Investors cannot directly invest in an index.

The principal risks are generally those attributable to bond investing. Holdings are subject to market, issuer, credit, prepayment, extension and other risks, and their values may fluctuate. Market risk is the risk that securities may decline in value due to factors affecting the securities markets or particular industries. Issuer risk is the risk that the value of a security may decline for reasons specific to the issuer, such as changes in its financial condition. The strategy may invest in mortgage-related securities, which can be paid off early if the borrowers on the underlying mortgages pay off their mortgages sooner than scheduled. data.title: Investing In iQIYI: A Contrarian Bet With Big Upside
data.description:
Well, it’s safe to say that the stock market has seen better days. The tariff-induced market crash that we’ve seen over the past week has been unprecedented, with Mr.Market scratching its proverbial head. However, if you’re a Warren Buffett fan, you’ve probably heard his famous quote in, 'Be greedy when others are fearful'; which fits perfectly in the current stock market setup. Truth be told, this could be a rare chance to pick up some high-quality stocks at a huge bargain.

Keeping that in mind, I believe China’s Netflix (NFLX), iQIYI (NASDAQ:IQ), that’s trading at a buck fifty right at the time of writing, is well worth exploring. Though its underlying business faces its fair share of challenges, its throwaway price at this point is tough to ignore. On top of that, the strength in the Chinese market and its pivot toward consumption-driven growth, adds another unique dynamic. Moreover, based on my fair value model, which takes into account relatively conservative growth estimates, shows a sizeable double-digit upside from current levels. Hence, for those with a decent risk appetite, IQ stock is an attractive value-oriented play at this time.

iQIYI At A Glance

iQIYI is a top Chinese video streaming play, that’s often referred to as the “Netflix of China”. Like its big brother, iQIYI offers a dizzying array of movies, TV shows, anime, and original Chinese dramas, and has efficiently carved out a powerful presence in its niche. However, unlike its alter ego, it also operates a freemium subscription, which offers a ton of content e with ads at low resolution. The dual approach allows the platform to cater to a much larger, generating strong sales through advertising and subscription fees.

Though it stopped posting subscriber numbers in Q2 last year, it's known to have amassed a subscriber base of around 100 million. Subscribers numbers reportedly peaked in Q1 2023 following the smashing success of its hit drama The Knockout, which pushed its subscribers to 129 million. However, recent results have shown sluggishness in this department, which remains a critical challenge.

The past few years for iQIYI have been mostly about breaking even and building on its bottom-line strength. Truth be told, a lot of that mirrors Netflix’s playbook, which includes cracking down on password sharing, raising subscription prices, while expanding ad sales. Also, the company has explored overseas expansion, especially in Southeast Asian territories, along with a wide variety of licensing deals. The results of these efforts have been two full years of profitability on a net income basis, with its net income and EBITDA margins at 2.5% and 33% respectively.

Naturally, the company has improved a great deal on the liquidity front, with a healthy $600 million in cash on hand to manage operational and interest obligations. Its equity-to-asset ratio of 0.29 times comfortably tops the industry median of 0.19, suggesting greater importance of equity in financing assets. Additionally, its debt-to-equity ratio of around 1.06, while slightly below the sector median of 1.27, suggests it's moderately leveraged.

Furthermore, a snapshot of its profitability metrics above points to an improving picture compared to its past averages and sector norms. For instance, its gross margin of roughly 25.2%, comfortably eclipses its five-year average of 16.4%. Similarly, its EBITDA margin of 33%, is comfortably ahead of the sector median of roughly 20% highlighting robust operational leverage and effective cost control. Also, its net income margin of 2.6%, though lagging the sector median, has swung from negative territory to positive over time.

Recent Operating Results

iQIYI’s operating results over the past few quarters have been mostly patchy, especially the last two where it missed top-line estimates by sizeable margins.

Its Q4 results showed revenues of $906 million, which fell short of consensus estimates of $913.55 million, while its non-GAAP EPADS was in line with estimates at a negative one cent. Membership services sales dropped 15% YOY $562.1 million, primarily due to a lighter content slate. Additionally, its advertising services segment wasn’t any better, which also fell 13% YOY to $196.4 million. Moreover, content distribution sales dropped by 20% on an annualized basis, landing at $55.7 million.

According to CEO Yu Gong, the company results were mostly off in Q4 on the back of a lighter content slate. However, he states that from late November though, new releases have led to an increase in subscriptions and user engagement numbers. In a nutshell, the company’s focus can be explained by a multi-layered strategy that is underpinned by premium content, innovative membership options, and expansion into different regions and formats.

In terms of content, its recent titles in the suspense/crime genre have been particularly pertinent, with its Light On Theater brand boasting standout titles like We Are Criminal Police and Drifting Away. Both of these brands surpassed the 10,000 mark in iQIYI’s popularity index, giving the platform a defining edge in the suspense category. Simultaneously, the platform’s female-oriented content the Love On Theater brand has thrived, as Fangs of Fortune and Love Song in Winter both broke past the 9,000 mark on the popularity index. Moreover, the platform boasts a leadership position in online movies, where it held the top spot in terms of viewership for the past 12 consecutive quarters with shows like HAHAHAHAHA and The Rap of China.

On the membership end, it introduced an overhauled tier structure, including individual plans for cost-conscious users and family plans offering a more premium offering. The goal is to accommodate users with varied budgets while reducing password sharing through new policies like “Extra Member”.

Another key development for iQIYI is its foray into mini-dramas, short vertical videos that typically last under three minutes. The goal is to broaden the platform’s user funnel by attracting viewers looking for short and snackable formats. Also, the management touts the platform’s existing technology and industry relationships, enabling it to efficiently stock more than 10,000 mini-dramas, while securing deals with 95% of top mini-drama content providers. To put things in perspective, the mini-drama segment has already shown a ton of top-line growth potential, with one title exceeding RMB 1 million in monthly revenue-sharing income.

Furthermore, iQIYI is actively seeking overseas expansion. It has found remarkable success with its Chinese-language content in new markets like Thailand, Australia, and Hong Kong, and is now spreading its tentacles in the Middle East, Latin America, and Brazil. Perhaps, a big part of its expansion plans hinges on its partnerships with major telecom carriers to facilitate subscriber bundling. On top of that, it's leveraging AI and virtual production to efficiently streamline content production while reducing costs at the same time. Its robust smart tools efficiently optimize scheduling, asset utilization, and script development through a novel production management system.

Discussing IQ Stock’s Valuation

It’s been a forgettable year, to say the least for IQ stock, which is down almost 40% in the past six months. In the past month alone, it's lost almost 30% of its value, which says a lot about the choppiness in the stock market.

Nonetheless, in assessing its fair value, I ran a free cash flow analysis to estimate what IQ stock be worth at this point. In the valuation model, the stock’s WACC is estimated at 5.83%, based on key assumptions over its cost of equity and cost of debt. Using, a relatively low beta of 0.11, and an equity risk premium of 5%, the cost of equity came in at a relatively low 4.82%. Combining these equity and debt inputs, the debt weight stands at almost 59% while its equity weight is at 41.2%, which effectively yields an after-tax WACC of 5.83%.

Keeping that in mind, the model assumes a relatively conservative free cash flow growth estimate of 5% over the next decade applied to its current free cash flow per share of 28 cents. Also, with a 3% terminal growth rate, the model produces a discounted terminal value of $8.99. Summing up the discounted annual and terminal values, we have a fair value estimate of $11.54, which is incredibly higher than the stock’s current price.

If we were to tweak the model and use just a 3% FCF growth assumption, the fair value would drop to $9.91, which is still head-and-shoulders above its current market price. Overall, though, it's important to know that the valuation model rests on key assumptions that iQIYI will be able to sustain its free cash flow growth while maintaining a modest cost of capital.

Risks To The Thesis

Despite the stock’s attractiveness, there are some key areas of concern which warrant attention.

Perhaps a key concern for skeptics is the likely escalation in competition from mini-dramas, particularly on platforms like ByteDance’s Douyin and Tencent. These companies have powerful ecosystems and robust algorithms that facilitate rapid scaling of short-format content while poaching iQIYI’s potential subscribers. Also, despite iQIYI having a larger percentage of mini-drama content providers, the lack of a quality-driven approach will dilute content quality over time.

Moreover, as recent developments have shown, iQIYI continues leaning on debt offerings, most recently $350 million worth of senior notes due at the end of the current decade. On the flipside, iQIYI’s frequent equity issuances continue pushing total shares outstanding toward the $970 million mark, raising major shareholder dilution concerns. Additionally, if economic conditions continue moving downhill, it will impact iQIYI’s ability to manage these obligations profitably.

Takeaway On IQ Stock

iQIYI stock has taken a hammering on the back of trade-war-driven market rout. Hence, now it's trading dirt cheap, and with what could potentially be a successful pivot toward premium content, promising mini-dramas, and overseas expansion, we could see a healthy upside from current levels, especially if it can effectively sustain free cash flow growth. Additionally, my fair value valuation model, which is based on conservative estimates, points to a fair value that dwarfs its current price. Hence, those who can stomach the near-term volatility should bet on IQ stock, which currently offers a compelling high-reward opportunity, along with notable downside risks.

data.title: InPost S.A.: Strong Moat, Clear Growth Runway, Cheap Valuation
data.description:
Investment summary

My recommendation for InPost S.A. (OTCPK:INPOY) (OTCPK:INPOF) is a buy rating. I believe the business has clearly demonstrated that once scale is reached, the unit economics of this model become highly attractive. 4Q24 results confirm Poland is still delivering strong volumes and profits, while the UK and Italy continue to scale, with meaningful operating leverage now showing up in the numbers. With a strong competitive advantage, clear growth runway, and cheap valuation, I think INPOY is an attractive buy.

Business Overview

INPOY is a European e-commerce logistics platform with a presence in eight cross-border markets. The business has an interesting delivery model called out-of-home [OOH] delivery. Basically, INPOY operates Automated Parcel Machines [APMs] and Pick-Up Drop-Off [PUDO] points. Through the usage of these, customers can deliver/collect parcels from a convenient network of lockers or designated locations vs. the traditional door-to-door delivery. As of 4Q24, INPOY managed ~47k APMs and ~34k PUDO points.

Solid Competitive Advantages

What I really like about INPOY is its scale advantage. On a standalone basis, this is a relatively commoditized business that almost everyone can enter, but the barrier is not at the entry level but at the scale level. As one scales the business, it is able to reduce the fixed cost of each APM/PUDO due to higher volume, which means the unit economics get better. And with that, one will have more financial capacity to quickly penetrate across other white spaces. This is exactly what INPOY did, and we can see from its Poland operations that reaching the point of substantial scale (an APM footprint that reaches ~90% of the urban population within a 7-minute walk) first translates to having the highest market share.

As a new entrant, you would face a significant cost disadvantage as volume will be less, and prime real estate locations will also be less available (since INPOY would most likely have already gone for them). And if they did, there is little reason for the real estate owner to have another similar offering in the area. Moreover, from the consumer standpoint, once they have the habit of picking the option of “collecting with INPOY APM/PUDO,” they would naturally use it again since it is reliable and works. Hence, this consumer behavior “lock-in” effect shouldn’t be underestimated.

INPOY's advantage extends beyond just its infrastructure base. The second leg of INPOY's competitive advantage is its direct relationships with large merchants, e-commerce marketplace platforms, SMEs, and retailers. INPOY effectively offers these customers a cheap and reliable alternative to solve their delivery/logistical issues, especially for sub-scale customers (if you have scale, you could probably justify having your own fleet to deliver, but not sub-scale players). Obviously, these customers want to work with the vendor that has the widest and deepest reach, as they want to cover as much delivery space as possible. So long as INPOY doesn’t mess up with its operations, this direct relationship should continue to remain healthy, giving little reason for these customers to switch to an alternative solution provider.

4Q24 results highlight Poland’s continuous growth and UK/Italy momentum

As mentioned, Poland is an INPOY more mature market, and it continues to deliver solid growth. In 4Q24, INPOY Poland saw 20% y/y parcel volume growth, reaching 209.8 million, with revenue up 15% y/y to ~PLN1.87 billion, while adj. EBITDA grew 19.2% y/y to PLN879.5 million. The notable aspect here is that the EBITDA margin expanded (from 45.5% in 4Q23 to 47.2% in 4Q24) as the business scaled, which supports my view above that scale leads to lower unit cost. While management has guided for slower growth in FY25, I don’t think it represents a structural slowdown in growth. I view it as management being cautious due to how the global economy is developing (impacted by Trump’s tariff plans). Poland’s ​e-commerce growth outlook is still fairly attractive. Per Statista, e-commerce revenue in Poland is expected to reach ~$35.8 billion by 2029, which represents a CAGR of ~9.66% from 2025 to 2029. Additionally, the number of e-commerce users in Poland is expected to grow to 26 million by 2029, which reflects increasing consumer adoption.

Even if Poland’s growth starts to slow, it is still a very cash-generative business that provides INPOY cash to fund its next growth engine—the UK and Italy. In the 4Q24 quarter, parcel volumes in this segment surged 56% y/y, reaching 34.5 million, and the notable part is that revenue more than doubled to PLN587.7 million, representing a 151% y/y growth. This tells us that INPOY was able to not only capture more volume but also improve effective pricing (through better product mix). The latter, about better product mix, is important because it tells us that customers are finding value in INPOY service and, hence, are willing to adopt higher-priced services. The drop-through to adj. EBITDA margin is even more notable as adj. EBITDA surged from PLN7 million in 4Q23 to PLN111.3 million—marking a massive 1,490% jump. As a result, segment EBITDA margins expanded to 18.9%, up from 3%​. This, again, supports my logic that scale leads to lower unit cost and better profitability.

For the UK/Italy, the growth runway is even better than Poland, given that INPOY is less mature in these markets (so they can grow organically via ecommerce volume growth and market share gains).

In the UK, execution has been great so far. The InPost app now has a 4.9-star rating on both iOS and Android, with 1.9 million downloads. The newly opened depot in Warrington has added national capacity and translated into improved service levels in the northwest, while the acquisition of Menzies further enhanced INPOY's logistics reach at the national level. As for Italy, while this is a smaller piece of the business, performance was also solid. Parcel volumes grew 47% y/y in 4Q24, with 7.2 million parcels delivered. Revenue grew accordingly by 56% y/y to PLN 93.6 million, driven largely by the B2C segment.

Valuation

At the current valuation of just 7x forward EBITDA, I believe the risk-reward is very attractive. Using management’s FY25 revenue guidance as a baseline for FY26/27 growth, I forecasted 20% CAGR through FY27, which equates to a revenue figure of PLN18.9 billion in FY27. I did not assume any further adj. EBITDA margin expansion post FY25 (management’s adj. EBITDA growth guide implies modest margin expansion in FY25) as I believe INPOY will need to allocate more cost to scale up its UK/Italy business. With these assumptions, I expect INPOY to generate PLN6.4 billion in adj. EBITDA. I don’t see a reason why INPOY should trade at the low end of its past three-year trading range—at 7.2x, it is the lowest it has ever traded over the past 3 years. While business growth is unlikely to regain back to mid-20%, such a steep discount from the average of ~10x is not justified, in my opinion. I believe valuation should be closer to 9x (the midpoint between the -1 standard deviation and the average).

Risk

If Poland's e-commerce market slows for an extended period of time, it will have a heavy impact on INPOY because it is the cash cow of INPOY. Given that international expansion is capital intensive, lower cash flow from Poland could limit INPOY's overall growth. In addition, while I think it is an irrational move, some competitors may decide to bite the bullet and endure years of cash burn to create a competitive model vs. INPOY.

Conclusion

My view for INPOY is a buy rating. This is a business with strong competitive advantage and has shown solid execution so far. At just 7x forward EBITDA, I think the market is underappreciating the long-term growth runway and the structural moat INPOY has built.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Muhlenkamp Q1 2025 Quarterly Letter
data.description:
Fellow Investors,

In many respects the first quarter of 2025 was little changed from the recent past. For example, inflation, as measured by the Consumer Price Index (CPI), was 2.8% on February 28, 2025, right in the middle of the 2.4% - 3.4% range it established in 2024. Similarly, the yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury was 4.35% on March 26, 2025, again in the middle of the 4.0% - 4.5% range that’s been in place since late 2023. Unemployment was 4.1% on February 28, 2025, the same level we saw in June, September, October, and December of ’24. Even the Federal Reserve left well enough alone and didn’t make any changes to the Federal Funds Rate in the quarter.

So, a pretty “ho hum” quarter. Not much to talk about, should make for a short memo, right?

Well, no.

There has been plenty of excitement in the government policy arena, mostly centered around tariffs and regulations. President Trump has imposed a number of tariffs against a broad array of countries and industries and promises to impose more. The specifics can vary on a daily, or even hourly basis. The President appears to have three goals: encourage businesses to invest in the U.S. creating a renaissance in manufacturing, raise revenue for the government, and use the threat of tariffs as leverage to get agreements in other areas of foreign policy. The sudden imposition of tariffs and the rapid changes have created chaos with businesses as they do their best to keep input costs low and make long-term plans. We think the rapidly changing tariff policy has contributed to stock market volatility and weakness in business and consumer sentiment surveys. From an economic perspective, higher taxes (which is all tariffs are) reduce overall economic activity and prosperity. They are also not new. Countries are often only willing to open their markets to foreigners if they get some of the jobs that come with the product. That’s why there is a Honda plant in Ohio, a BMW plant in South Carolina, and a Tesla plant in both Germany and China. It does mark a significant shift in U.S. policy, and it will take time for businesses to adjust. There is also the fear that a global “trade war” will fuel a new depression just like the Smoot-Hawley tariffs did in 1930. We think the likelihood of that outcome is remote as the context in which the tariffs are being imposed are very, very different today. Finally, some are concerned that the tariffs will stoke inflation. We have no opinion on whether these tariffs will increase inflation or not. We have plenty of other reasons to remain concerned about a resurgence of inflation, which remains high on our list of things to watch for.

Equally as interesting as the change in tariffs are the efforts by the new administration to restructure the government itself and reduce the regulatory burden it imposes on the country. The restructuring has captured the most media attention, particularly the Department of Government Efficiency’s (DOGE’s) “shock and awe” campaign against the bloated bureaucracy. We applaud the top to bottom effort to reduce waste and fraud and hope that when mistakes are made, as they inevitably will be, they are quickly corrected. Little noted by the media, but we think of enormous importance are the announcements by the EPA (Environmental Protection Agency) and FCC (Federal Communications Commission) to relook all of their regulations from top to bottom (including the EPA’s “Endangerment Finding” which concluded greenhouse gasses including carbon dioxide pose a threat to public health and welfare) and a Presidential Order requiring the elimination of 10 regulations for every new one imposed. The EPA announcement in particular caught our attention, and we realized that we must re-examine all of our investments to make sure we understand the impact a significant change in regulations would pose – both threats and opportunities. That process is ongoing and has already resulted in the sale of one holding.

In the stock market we are seeing a rotation away from the darlings of the last two years. So far that rotation has generally worked to our advantage. We remain concerned about the possibility of a recession as some recent consumer data has come in weaker than expected and businesses are cautious given the changing rules as described above. As always, we continue to search for profitable investment opportunities and will put money to work when we find them.

If you have any questions please get in touch with us, we’d love to hear from you. With our best wishes for your continued success and good health in 2025,

Jeff Muhlenkamp, Portfolio Manager | Muhlenkamp & Company, Inc.

Ron Muhlenkamp, Founder | Muhlenkamp & Company, Inc.

CPI- The Consumer Price Index measures the average change in prices over time that consumers pay for a basket of goods and services, commonly known as inflation. One cannot invest directly in an index.

data.title: Power Integrations: Efficiency Is The Ultimate Clean Energy
data.description:
Power Integrations (POWI) is a unique company that we have been following for some time. We started with a "Sell" rating as we thought the company's valuation was excessive, and since then, shares have lost roughly half their value. However, we probably became optimistic too soon, seeing shares as attractively priced late last year, but shares have underperformed the market since then. Still, the underperformance against the iShares Global Clean Energy ETF (ICLN), and in particular the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), has been less dramatic.

As a reminder, Power Integrations is a small company with big ambitions. It is widely considered the global leader in Gallium Nitride (GaN) high-voltage power conversion. This technology can be significantly more efficient compared to silicon, and in terms of cost it is approaching cost parity. For this reason, it is believed that GaN technology is the future of high-voltage power conversion, and Power Integrations controls some of the key IP.

High-voltage is critical if you want fast-charging, which is why many mobile phone chargers now use integrated circuits from Power Integrations. While we do not know if BYD (BYDDF) is using GaN technology to charge its EVs in five minutes, it was shared that the charging power was 1,000 kilowatts. This likely means using very high voltage.

Power Integrations can tailor their technology to make purpose-built solutions for a wide range of applications. These include smartphone chargers, LEDs, electric locomotives, motor drives, and electric vehicles. Power Integrations introduced 900 V and 1250 V products in 2023, and 1700 V in 2024. The higher voltages should unlock new applications and more impressive performance.

Attractive Industry

Power Integrations is a niche player in an already attractive industry of analogue chips. One reason the industry is attractive is the high switching costs customers would incur if they decide to replace one of Power Integrations’ chips from their designs. They would have to spend considerable time and engineering resources re-designing and re-validating the products, and adds risks that the new version might include error triggering product recalls. This means that once one of Power Integrations’ chips is added to a product design it tends to stay in the bill of materials. One statistic the company shared to back this up is that 52% of its 2024 sales came from product families introduced before 2002.

Financials

Power Integrations has a good track record of revenue and earnings growth, but there was an enormous demand pull-forward during the COVID pandemic when people decided to splurge on new appliances, electronics, and other consumer goods. The normalization has been painful for the company, but there were recent signs that the company was returning to growth. Of course, this was before tariff disruptions, so it remains to be seen if the nascent recovery is short-circuited by recent developments.

Taking a longer-term view, Power Integrations has a good track record of growing revenue at a faster pace compared to the analogue chip industry, with a CAGR roughly 2% higher. Its 2024 revenue was similar to that of 2019 given the demand pull-forward, but we do expect growth to return at some point as the company launches new innovative and more capable chips, and as many of its end-markets are growing rapidly.

Shareholder Returns

Power Integrations pays a small dividend, but it has been increasing and at current prices yields almost 2%. The company also increased the dividend by 5% at the end of last year. Shares outstanding have declined, down close to 13% since 2008, as the company has repurchased shares opportunistically at times when it has considered they were trading at attractive valuations.

For example, in 2022 it spent roughly twice its free cash flow in share repurchases, significantly more than what it spent on the dividend or previous share buybacks.

In November 2024, Power Integrations announced a new $50 million repurchase authorization. It is one of the companies we have seen take more decisive action with respect to repurchases when valuations appear attractive, and it tends to significantly reduce buybacks once the share price recovers.

Balance Sheet

One reason Power Integrations has been able to react so strongly with significant buybacks when the share price has decreased is the flexibility provided by its solid balance sheet. The company has basically no long-term debt, and ended 2024 with roughly $300 million in cash and short-term investments.

Short-term Headwinds

There are good reasons why the share price has been under pressure for the last couple of years. As already mentioned, part of it was the resulting weakness after demand was pull-forward during the pandemic boom for consumer appliances. There were signs the company was starting to grow revenue once again, although that will be put to the test as the result of the trade war.

Another temporary headwind is that current market conditions have resulted in the Japanese Yen (FXY) appreciating given its haven currency characteristics. Power Integrations benefits from a weaker Yen, as it lowers its manufacturing costs, resulting in higher gross margins. In addition, there is uncertainty as to the potential cost impact that tariffs on Japan could have on production costs.

Long-term Tailwinds

While the short to medium term looks complicated for the company, especially if the announced tariffs remain in place for a prolonged period of time, we remain optimistic about the long-term prospects for the company.

For example, its serviceable addressable market [SAM] is expected to double by 2027 compared to where it was in 2022. The company is also further diversifying into new end-markets, many of which have strong megatrends in their favor. For example, there is an important opportunity for the company with electric vehicles, an end-market expected to grow around the world, but also an opportunity where the company believes it can increase dollar content per unit from tens of dollars to over $100 per vehicle. Other attractive end-markets include micromobility applications like e-bikes and e-scooters, smart appliances, and building automation.

Perhaps one of the biggest specific opportunities lies in helping power data centers, which have on average 2.5 power supplies per server. Given the increased power needs for AI workloads this opens new opportunities for GaN solutions.

Future Outlook

While the short term to medium term continues to look complicated for the company, we continue to believe this company has attractive long-term prospects. It might require patience, especially if the U.S. economy enters recession or if the tariff war continues to escalate. Still, we believe the important high-voltage semiconductor niche is being underappreciated by investors. High-voltage solutions will be critical for increased energy efficiency, and to enable many technologies required for a lower carbon future.

The cleanest energy is the one that is not wasted, which is why efficient energy consumption and transmission is so important. At the same time, as GaN technology continues to improve, with higher voltage ICs and lower cost, we expect new applications to be unlocked and for the potential market to continue expanding.

Valuation

At first look, Power Integrations appears expensive, but there are two important factors to remember. One is that profitability has been significantly pressured by lower revenues as things normalize after the Covid demand pull-forward.

Compared to historical peak earnings, current prices would translate to a ~15x Price/Earnings multiple. Another factor to consider is that the company has a balance sheet with a net cash position amounting to ~$5 per share.

In fact, compared to other analogue chip peers, Power Integrations appears attractively priced on a Price/Sales ratio. The company currently has a similar forward Price/Earnings multiple to Texas Instruments (TXN), but still trades at a premium compared to Analog Devices (ADI). Still, we believe Power Integrations deserves a premium valuation given the attractiveness of the GaN power semiconductor chips niche.

Risks

The biggest risk we currently see is a potential recession in the U.S., which would significantly reduce demand for appliances and consumer products. The other major risk is the potential production cost impact if tariffs on Japan are maintained at current levels. These risks are mitigated by a strong balance sheet, and significant end-market diversification. Roughly 35% of revenue comes from the industrial and auto segment, 39% from consumer devices, 14% from computers, and 12% from communications which is mostly mobile phone chargers.

Conclusion

Power Integrations is an under-followed small-cap company operating in the already attractive industry of analogue chips, and specialized in the promising niche of GaN power devices. While the company is facing short-term and medium term headwinds, its long-term prospects remain attractive with many secular trends in its favor. There are important risks to consider, but these are mitigated by end-market diversification, high customer switching costs, and a very strong balance sheet. Using trailing-twelve-month valuation metrics, shares appear expensive, but on a forward basis and adjusting for the net cash per share, they look significantly more attractively priced.

data.title: Artisan Developing World Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Dear Fellow Shareholder:

Market Backdrop

The Artisan Developing World Fund (MUTF:ARTYX, Investor Class) returned 1.98% for the quarter ended March 31, 2025, versus 2.93% for the MSCI Emerging Markets Index (all returns in USD unless stated otherwise). Since June 30, 2015, the Artisan Developing World Fund has returned 155.27% cumulatively, versus 42.90% for the MSCI Emerging Markets Index. The quarter began with perceived US economic momentum and notions of US exceptionalism. Markets were then uprooted by China’s artificial intelligence achievements, global tariff uncertainty, and an end to the so-called German debt brake. Chinese markets were standout performers (MSCI China Index +15.02%) against a backdrop of tariffs and geopolitical tensions, as a Chinese hedge fund released an efficient AI reasoning model (DEEPSEEK) that underscored the appeal of China’s technology capabilities. This model achievement also led investors to question the prodigious sums being spent on AI capital formation in the US. As a result, the S&P 500® Index (SP500, SPX) declined 4.27% during the quarter, while the tech-heavy Taiwanese market declined 12.63% (MSCI Taiwan Index). Exacerbating this dynamic, Germany responded to uncertainty over the US commitment to Ukraine by releasing the debt brake to invest in both military capabilities and depleted domestic infrastructure. The MSCI ACWI ex USA Index rose 5.23% as investors flocked to European banks and other perceived beneficiaries of European fiscal largesse.

Currencies also reflected an increased preference for non-US assets (MSCI EM Currency Index +1.74%, euro +4.32%, yen +5.10%). From a domestic EM standpoint, Brazil rose (MSCI Brazil Index +14.05%) despite increasingly tight monetary conditions, perhaps as investors saw levered plays on non-US exposure and Brazil made progress on a Mercosur-European trade deal. Central and Eastern European markets rose as investors similarly sought disproportionate beneficiaries of European fiscal dynamics (MSCI Poland Index +31.27%, MSCI Czech Republic Index +28.66%). South Africa rose (MSCI South Africa Index +13.84%), likely along similar lines. Weaker markets included Indonesia, as President Prabowo’s signature free meal policy created discomfort about the country’s fiscal trajectory and productivity outlook. Turkey also declined (MSCI Turkey Index -8.96%) as the government arrested Istanbul’s mayor, who is a key opposition figure.

Contributors and Detractors

Top contributors to performance for the quarter included Southeast Asian e-commerce and gaming platform Sea (SE), Latin American online platform company MercadoLibre (MELI), Chinese online recruitment services company Kanzhun (BZ), Chinese Internet and technology company Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY), and electronic payments network Visa (V). Sea benefited from accelerating growth and monetization in e-commerce, renewed promise in its gaming business, and strong on and off-platform development in financial services.

MercadoLibre experienced sustained momentum in its e-commerce business due to gross merchandise value (GMV) growth and take rate realization, and good fintech development as credit expansion continued while provisions moderated. Kanzhun benefited from improving recruitment demand and optimism around the impact of AI on both job postings and its own operations. Tencent benefited from renewed investor confidence in Chinese technology companies following the release of DeepSeek, especially given Tencent’s strong communications ecosystem, cloud capabilities, and distribution advantages. Visa benefited from reaccelerating volume growth and stronger-than-expected cross- border performance, and perhaps from elevated inflation and nominal spending.

Bottom contributors to performance for the quarter included Indian food delivery and quick commerce company Zomato, Australian logistics services software company WiseTech Global (OTCPK:WTCHF), graphics semiconductor company Nvidia (NVDA), Indian online travel company MakeMyTrip (MMYT), and semiconductor design and simulation company Cadence (CDNS). Zomato declined amid weaker consumer sentiment in India and concerns about the increasingly competitive environment in its “Quick Commerce” segment, against the backdrop of continued capital-raising activity from competitors.

WiseTech fell due to governance concerns after the resignation of independent board directors related to disputes with the company’s founder, and delays in new product introductions.

Nvidia fell as DeepSeek’s strong efficiency gains catalyzed fears of industry oversupply, and Nvidia became a proxy for US exceptionalism. MakeMyTrip fell due to concerns about the slowdown of consumer sentiment in India, despite continued resilience in domestic and outbound travel. Cadence declined due to signs of sustained industry pressure in its core digital and analog semiconductor design business and continued cautious commentary around its China business due to geopolitical constraints.

Market Outlook

Policy uncertainty continues to permeate markets. The dollar (USDOLLAR,DXY) has recently weakened against most currencies. Investors have been left to contemplate the possibility that US exceptionalism has peaked, and that industrial policy could weaken the dollar and US markets further. At the same time, it is notable that the MSCI Emerging Markets ex China Index declined 1.72% during this quarter. The reality is that the US is an engine for growth and key trading partner for many emerging markets countries. To the extent that the US lowers interest rates to support economic growth, real rates suggest only limited scope for interest rate reductions in many emerging countries. Moreover, we have long emphasized that lower rates are not a panacea for economic development in most emerging countries, which suffer from a lack of domestic capital formation and foreign investment. In fact, regardless of the price of money (i.e., the level of interest rates), the US’ increased focus on domestic priorities and Europe’s pivot toward defense could mean that capital formation in emerging markets is even more elusive in the coming years. To the extent that capital formation is the key constraint on investment-led growth, job creation, and real income growth in emerging markets, broader economic participation in these countries may remain elusive. China does have some potential to fill this gap in emerging markets as an exporter of capital and technology, but its domestic priorities may take precedent, especially over shorter periods. More realistically, China may provide emerging markets with compelling products at competitive price points.

As for China, we have written extensively about China’s ecosystems for domestic capital formation, which are unique in an emerging markets context and perhaps only eclipsed globally by those of the United States. The DeepSeek achievement reflects the necessity of domestic technology innovation in China given Western export restrictions, and the engineering talent and capital resources in the country. Indeed, Chinese electronic vehicle pioneer BYD’s (OTCPK:BYDDF) recent achievements in fast EV charging and autonomous driving systems (which will be embedded for free in its cars) also reflect unique ecosystems for domestic capital formation. These ecosystems have the potential to flourish, even in the absence of access to Western technology. At the same time, China’s capital formation capability while palpable for key industrial priorities has largely been dormant the last few years, which is a key reason why the economy has decelerated so markedly. Thus, a key question surrounds the extent to which private capital formation accelerates in China, which would reduce the need for China to provide fiscal support and bolster property markets. While many companies in China have spoken recently of AI-driven strategies to increase revenues and reduce costs, the reality is that AI development is for now a more tangible concept than improved economic development. We have been careful to create and maintain pathways for reflation in China assets over the last several years while limiting our capital at risk if, for example, tensions with the US were to escalate.

Portfolio Positioning

We developed the concept of “Flexion” out of necessity, at a moment of crisis. It was a tool for us to add to positions in a contrarian way, while simultaneously allowing for a pathway to deemphasize that investment if necessary. In more recent iterations, we have defined Flexion in large part as follows: adding to positions with progression to a lower weight and trimming with progression to a higher weight. Practiced with patience and consistency, Flexion can become a tool for an asset allocation outcome. For example, over the last several years, we took capital back from Nvidia with progression to a higher or maximum weight. In the aggregate, we estimate that we have taken back approximately 1,800bps (net of buys) from our Nvidia investment since establishing our position in the first half of 2019. In recent months, as the US AI capital spending boom has been called into question, Nvidia has started to naturally underperform our portfolio. Exiting the quarter, it represented just 2.8% of our investments. While we have not yet endeavored to add to Nvidia with progression to a lower weight, the point is that this process has been occurring naturally, throughout our portfolio. Thus, while we do not feel qualified to call for an end to US exceptionalism (nor would we have predicted it to begin with), our asset allocation over a period of time has begun to reflect precisely this possibility— even as we add selectively to US Passporters at the margin.

Notably, of our top 10 holdings, Visa (which we own primarily for correlation benefits) and CrowdStrike (CRWD) are the only US-based Passporters. Three years ago, there were 6 US-based Passporters in our top 10. Similarly, we are positioned to allow for some progression to a higher weight in our China holdings, while taking capital back if this occurs. Ultimately, our positioning for an end to US exceptionalism, or alternatively a continuation of it, is to a large degree an outcome of movements within the portfolio. If an investment or country allocation deteriorates beyond our expectations, we have established a pathway to deemphasize that investment or country.

Last quarter, we articulated an emphasis on EM assets exposed to large population clusters with access to credit and formal employment that can benefit disproportionately from consumption. It is this alignment that creates the potential for revenue velocity despite economic limitations, which when paired with attractive incremental margin structures can create the potential for disproportionate equity outcomes. For example, Sea has been a successful investment not because of rapid growth in the middle class, but rather because young employed tech-savvy people have capable smartphones that they are using to buy basic goods and in many cases access credit for the first time outside the banking system. MakeMyTrip has been conducive to disproportionate equity outcomes in part because formally employed workers are aspiring to domestic travel, and because large and wealthy niche populations in India are increasingly traveling abroad. By contrast, a narrow focus on domicile can implicitly hold investors captive to a production-driven construct (i.e., capital formation in the emerging markets with limited potential for convergence). By contrast, consumption growth tied to the modern economy and attractive population clusters can significantly exceed the pace of EM capital formation. Notably, three of our top four performers since inception are domiciled in the emerging markets, and approximately two thirds of our performance since inception has come from EM-domiciled companies. In other words, our willingness to look beyond production-driven constraints embedded in the domicile construct has been a key determinant of the success of our EM-domiciled investments. Moreover, our Passport company exposure provides additional conduits to Western ecosystems for capital formation, aspirational products and foundational technologies. These products and services, in turn, hold appeal to the aforementioned population clusters that permeate the emerging world.

We thank you for your trust and confidence.

Lewis S. Kaufman, CFA

Investment Process

We seek to build, preserve and enhance a stream of compounded business value. We define this emphasis as follows:

Build: Pair low penetration domestic demand with scalable and enduring businesses that are able to drive value creation and disproportionate outcomes.

Preserve: Preserve value creation and establish a forward-looking construct for managing risk.

Enhance: Leverage value pathways to enhance long-term value creation.
